heroOverview: Congenital asplenia is the absence of the spleen from birth, caused by a failure of splenic development during embryogenesis. It is rare — population-based estimates suggest it affects fewer than 1 in 10,000 live births when isolated, though it is more common as part of heterotaxy syndromes where internal organ siting is abnormal. The spleen is the body's main filter for encapsulated bacteria and damaged red blood cells, so children and adults born without one face a lifelong markedly elevated risk of overwhelming post-splenectomy infection (OPSI), a rapidly fatal septicaemia that can kill within 24–48 hours. With early diagnosis, vaccination against encapsulated organisms, antibiotic prophylaxis in childhood, and patient education, most affected individuals lead full active lives, but vigilance must be maintained permanently.

keyStats.prevalence: Isolated congenital asplenia affects approximately 1 in 10,000–20,000 live births; it is more common within heterotaxy syndromes (situs ambiguus), which occur in about 1 in 10,000 births (British Society for Haematology, 2022)
keyStats.demographics: Affects males and females equally when isolated; heterotaxy-associated asplenia has a slight male predominance in some series
keyStats.avgAge: Diagnosis is ideally made in the neonatal period; OPSI risk is highest in infancy and early childhood but persists lifelong
keyStats.globalCases: Precise global prevalence is unknown; congenital asplenia is recognised globally in all ethnic populations

definition: Congenital asplenia (ICD-10: Q89.01) is the congenital absence of the spleen, resulting from complete failure of splenic organogenesis during the fifth to eighth week of fetal development. It may occur in isolation — associated with mutations in the RPSA gene (ribosomal protein SA), which encodes a component of the 40S ribosomal subunit essential for splenic progenitor survival — or as part of right isomerism (bilateral right-sidedness) in heterotaxy syndromes, where other serious cardiac, pulmonary, and abdominal situs abnormalities coexist. Polysplenia (multiple small spleens) is the left-isomerism counterpart. The spleen performs three essential immunological functions: filtration of the blood to remove encapsulated bacteria such as Streptococcus pneumoniae, Haemophilus influenzae type b, and Neisseria meningitidis; production of opsonising antibodies (especially IgM) that facilitate phagocytosis of capsulated organisms; and generation and maturation of marginal zone B cells responsible for T-independent antibody responses to polysaccharide antigens. Without a spleen, capsulated bacteria entering the bloodstream are not adequately cleared, producing the risk of OPSI — a bacteraemia that can overwhelm the host within hours. Congenital asplenia falls under the shared care of genetics, paediatric haematology, and (when cardiac defects coexist) paediatric cardiology and cardiac surgery.

typesClassification.description: Absence of the spleen with normal situs and organ arrangement; associated with autosomal dominant or de novo mutations in RPSA; the cardiac anatomy is usually normal, but infection risk is fully present.

Part of a syndrome of bilateral right-sidedness; complex congenital heart defects are almost universal and often life-threatening; both the spleen and the left-sided structures are absent or abnormal; requires urgent cardiac assessment in the newborn period.

typesClassification.type: Functional asplenia in setting of structural spleen
typesClassification.description: Not a congenital structural absence but a physiologically non-functioning spleen — as in sickle cell disease — which produces the same infection risks and management requirements, though it is coded separately.

primarySymptoms: Absence of the spleen on imaging or physical examination — congenital asplenia is usually asymptomatic until an infection occurs or it is found incidentally.
Howell-Jolly bodies on peripheral blood film — nuclear remnants in red blood cells normally removed by the spleen; their presence at any age indicates absent or severely impaired splenic function.
Elevated platelet count (thrombocytosis) and white cell count, common haematological findings in asplenia reflecting the spleen's normal role in platelet and leukocyte sequestration.
Sudden high fever — the first and often the only warning sign of OPSI, a septicaemia that can progress to shock within hours.
Rigors, severe headache, and vomiting, which may accompany the fever of an OPSI episode and can rapidly evolve into septic shock.
Features of the associated heterotaxy syndrome — cyanosis, respiratory distress, or murmur — in those with congenital heart defects.
Abdominal symptoms consistent with functional abnormalities — malrotation, biliary atresia, or other visceral anomalies that co-occur in heterotaxy-related asplenia.
Easy bruising or abnormal bleeding if thrombocytopenia is a co-occurring feature, particularly in heterotaxy-related disease.
earlyWarningSigns: Unexplained fever in a person known to have congenital asplenia — must be treated as a medical emergency until bacterial sepsis is excluded.
Incidental finding of Howell-Jolly bodies on a blood film performed for unrelated reasons, prompting imaging to confirm absent spleen.
Incidental finding of absent spleen on an abdominal ultrasound performed for unrelated reasons in a child or adult.
emergencySigns: Fever above 38°C in any person with congenital asplenia — constitutes a sepsis alert requiring immediate medical evaluation and empirical antibiotics within 1 hour.
Rapid deterioration to confusion, cold clammy skin, low blood pressure, or very fast heart rate — signs of septic shock that demand emergency treatment.
Petechiae or a rapidly spreading purpuric rash, which can signal meningococcal or pneumococcal septicaemia.
Any febrile illness in an asplenic infant under 2 years of age, as this age group is at highest risk of fulminant OPSI.

causes.description: Loss-of-function variants in RPSA (ribosomal protein SA, chromosome 3p22) impair the survival of splenic progenitor cells during the fifth to eighth week of embryogenesis; RPSA mutations account for approximately 10–20% of isolated congenital asplenia cases, following an autosomal dominant inheritance pattern with variable expressivity.

Failure of embryonic left-right axis determination produces bilateral right-sidedness — right isomerism — in which neither side of the developing embryo acquires a left-sided identity; since the spleen is a left-sided organ, it fails to develop, and complex cardiovascular malformations coexist because left-sided heart structures (pulmonary veins, morphological left ventricle) are also disrupted.

Mutations in genes regulating nodal signalling (NODAL, LEFTY2, FOXH1, ZIC3, CFC1) can disrupt the normal left-right patterning cascade during early embryogenesis, producing heterotaxy and associated asplenia; ZIC3 mutations are X-linked and account for a subset of male patients.

Motile cilia at the embryonic node drive the left-right signal by generating directional fluid flow; pathogenic variants in ciliopathy genes (DNAI1, DNAH5, and others) can cause random situs (situs inversus or heterotaxy), including asplenia, in approximately 0.5–1% of primary ciliary dyskinesia cases.

A proportion of congenital asplenia cases arise from de novo structural chromosomal abnormalities or epigenetic disruption during early fetal organogenesis, with no identifiable monogenic cause and no family history.

riskFactors.factor: Family history of congenital asplenia or heterotaxy
riskFactors.description: RPSA mutations follow autosomal dominant inheritance, so a first-degree relative with congenital asplenia or an RPSA variant raises the risk in offspring to approximately 50%; genetic counselling and antenatal screening are recommended.

riskFactors.factor: Known RPSA mutation in a parent
riskFactors.description: Antenatal exome or gene panel testing can identify fetal carriers when a parent carries a pathogenic RPSA variant; affected fetuses can then be planned for immediate neonatal investigation and prophylaxis.

riskFactors.factor: Heterotaxy or complex congenital heart disease in a sibling
riskFactors.description: Recurrence risk in heterotaxy syndromes varies by underlying gene: X-linked ZIC3 mutations confer a 50% risk in male offspring of carrier mothers; autosomal recessive ciliopathy genes confer 25% sibling recurrence.

Some animal-model data suggest that disruption of nodal signalling during the critical first-trimester window by teratogens can impair laterality, though the evidence in humans is limited and most cases lack identifiable environmental triggers.
De novo chromosomal variants associated with laterality defects are marginally more frequent with advancing maternal age, though the absolute risk increase is small.

affectedDemographics: Neonates and infants identified incidentally or via newborn screening programmes
Older children and adults with previously undiagnosed asplenia presenting with a first OPSI episode
Patients with heterotaxy and complex congenital heart disease evaluated by paediatric cardiology
Family members of a proband with an RPSA mutation undergoing predictive genetic testing
Individuals of any ancestry — congenital asplenia occurs in all ethnic groups worldwide
diagnosisOverview: Congenital asplenia is diagnosed by confirming the absence of splenic tissue, usually by abdominal ultrasound, and documenting the characteristic haematological signature. The peripheral blood film is central: Howell-Jolly bodies (nuclear remnants in red cells), target cells, and Pappenheimer bodies are reliably present when the spleen is absent or functionally silent. Splenic radionuclide scanning can confirm functional asplenia when ultrasound is equivocal. In heterotaxy syndromes, echocardiography is urgent to define the cardiac anatomy because complex heart defects drive early morbidity and mortality. Genetic testing — targeted RPSA sequencing for isolated cases, or a broader laterality gene panel for heterotaxy — establishes the molecular cause, enables familial risk assessment, and facilitates antenatal diagnosis in subsequent pregnancies. Newborn blood spot programmes in several countries now include Howell-Jolly body screening, though coverage is incomplete globally. The key clinical imperative after diagnosis is not further classification but immediate initiation of prophylaxis: vaccination and prophylactic antibiotics, because OPSI risk begins at birth.

diagnosticTests.purpose: The primary imaging test to confirm the absence of splenic tissue; also evaluates associated situs abnormalities (malrotation, biliary abnormalities) in heterotaxy.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A painless gel-based scan lasting 15–20 minutes; no preparation usually required in an infant; results are immediate.

diagnosticTests.test: Peripheral blood film for Howell-Jolly bodies
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects nuclear remnants in red blood cells that are normally filtered by the spleen, providing a reliable and inexpensive functional marker of asplenia.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw; the film is examined under a microscope and results are available within a few hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Essential in heterotaxy-associated asplenia to characterise complex congenital heart disease; guides surgical planning and prognosis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: An ultrasound of the heart via the chest; painless, lasting 30–60 minutes; often performed under sedation in infants.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms functional asplenia when imaging is equivocal; also used to detect accessory splenic tissue (splenunculi) that may provide partial function.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small amount of radioactive tracer is injected intravenously; a gamma camera images the abdomen over 30–60 minutes; radiation exposure is low.

diagnosticTests.test: RPSA gene sequencing / laterality gene panel
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the causative genetic variant for isolated or familial congenital asplenia, enabling familial risk counselling and antenatal testing.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood or saliva sample sent to a genetics laboratory; results take 2–8 weeks and are discussed with a clinical geneticist.

diagnosticTests.test: Full blood count and blood film
diagnosticTests.purpose: Documents thrombocytosis, leukocytosis, and red cell morphological changes (target cells, Howell-Jolly bodies) characteristic of hyposplenism.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard venous blood draw; results available within hours as part of routine laboratory analysis.

treatmentOverview: There is no treatment that creates or restores a spleen. Management of congenital asplenia therefore centres entirely on preventing the catastrophic infections that an absent spleen cannot defend against, and on responding rapidly when infection breaks through. The three pillars of management are: vaccination, antibiotic prophylaxis, and patient and family education. Vaccines against the three main encapsulated pathogens — Streptococcus pneumoniae (pneumococcus), Haemophilus influenzae type b, and Neisseria meningitidis — are given as early and as comprehensively as the immunisation schedule allows. Since asplenic individuals have a diminished antibody response to polysaccharide antigens, conjugate vaccines are strongly preferred over plain polysaccharide vaccines, and additional doses beyond the routine schedule are given. The pneumococcal conjugate series should be followed by a polysaccharide booster from age 2 years, with repeat boosters at least every 5 years. Meningococcal ACWY and B vaccines, Hib-MenC conjugate, and influenza vaccination are all recommended. Oral prophylactic penicillin V (amoxicillin in penicillin-allergic patients) is given daily throughout childhood and in most guidelines is continued lifelong — at minimum until the age of 16–18 and then as patient preference with appropriate counselling. Emergency standby antibiotics (oral amoxicillin-clavulanate or a fluoroquinolone) are prescribed and carried at all times. Patients must be educated that any fever above 38°C requires immediate medical attention and oral standby antibiotics, and that they should carry a medical alert card or wear a medical alert bracelet. Management of associated congenital heart disease or heterotaxy features requires specialist paediatric cardiology and cardiac surgery input and is the principal driver of early mortality in heterotaxy-related asplenia.

medicalTreatments.name: Pneumococcal conjugate and polysaccharide vaccination
medicalTreatments.description: The pneumococcal conjugate vaccine (PCV13 or PCV15/20) is given in the primary infant series followed by polysaccharide vaccine (PPSV23) from age 2 years and repeated every 5 years; this is the most important single preventive intervention, since Streptococcus pneumoniae causes approximately 50–70% of OPSI cases.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Conjugate vaccines reduce invasive pneumococcal disease by approximately 70–90% in the general population; response may be modestly reduced in asplenia but vaccination remains highly protective.

medicalTreatments.name: Prophylactic oral penicillin V (phenoxymethylpenicillin)
medicalTreatments.description: 125–250 mg twice daily throughout childhood, increasing to 250–500 mg twice daily after age 5; continued for life in most guidelines (British Society for Haematology); provides continuous protection against pneumococcal bacteraemia when vaccine coverage is incomplete.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces the incidence of pneumococcal bacteraemia by approximately 85% in prophylaxis-compliant asplenic children in landmark trials.

medicalTreatments.name: Meningococcal vaccination (ACWY and B)
medicalTreatments.description: Meningococcal conjugate ACWY vaccine given in the primary schedule and boosted every 5 years; MenB vaccine given as a series and boosted; N. meningitidis is the second most common cause of OPSI after pneumococcus.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Conjugate MenACWY vaccines reduce invasive meningococcal disease by more than 80% against serotypes covered in immunised populations.

medicalTreatments.name: Haemophilus influenzae type b (Hib) vaccination
medicalTreatments.description: Hib-containing conjugate vaccines are given in the standard infant series; asplenic children should receive an additional dose if primary series was incomplete or started late.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Near-elimination of invasive Hib disease (>95% reduction) has been achieved in countries with universal conjugate vaccination programmes.

medicalTreatments.name: Emergency standby antibiotics (amoxicillin-clavulanate)
medicalTreatments.description: A prescription supply kept at home for immediate self-administration at the first sign of fever (temperature ≥38°C); taken while seeking emergency medical care, not instead of it.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Prompt antibiotic initiation within 1 hour of fever onset is estimated to reduce OPSI mortality by 50% or more in asplenic patients based on retrospective data and expert consensus.

surgicalOptions.name: Cardiac surgery for associated congenital heart disease
surgicalOptions.description: In heterotaxy-associated asplenia, complex cardiac defects (total anomalous pulmonary venous drainage, single ventricle, dextrocardia, etc.) require staged surgical palliation or correction; timing and approach depend on the specific anatomy.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Outcomes vary widely by the complexity of the cardiac anatomy; single-ventricle palliation carries a 10-year survival of approximately 60–70% in major centres; biventricular repair, where feasible, has better long-term outcomes.

alternativeTreatments.name: Splenic autotransplantation (in splenectomy, not applicable here)
alternativeTreatments.description: Autotransplantation of splenic tissue is used after traumatic splenectomy; it is not applicable to congenital asplenia, where there is no tissue to transplant. It is mentioned to distinguish it from any misconception that it is an option in this setting.

alternativeTreatments.name: Medical alert identification
alternativeTreatments.description: Carrying a medical alert card and wearing a medical alert bracelet or watch are non-pharmacological but critically important interventions that ensure emergency clinicians are aware of asplenia and initiate antibiotics without delay.

alternativeTreatments.name: Annual influenza vaccination
alternativeTreatments.description: Influenza itself does not directly cause OPSI but predisposes to secondary bacterial pneumonia with encapsulated organisms; annual vaccination of asplenic patients and their household contacts is recommended.

whySeeSpecialist: A clinical geneticist should evaluate all patients with congenital asplenia to identify the molecular cause, counsel the family on recurrence risk, and arrange antenatal testing for future pregnancies. A paediatric haematologist or immunologist coordinates the vaccination and prophylaxis programme. Paediatric cardiology review is essential in heterotaxy-associated asplenia. All asplenic patients benefit from a specialist review at least annually to ensure vaccines are up to date, prophylactic antibiotics are being taken, and emergency plans are current.
doctorSelectionGuide: The managing team ideally includes a clinical geneticist familiar with splenic and laterality disorders, a paediatric or adult haematologist experienced in hyposplenism and OPSI prevention, and — when cardiac anomalies are present — a paediatric cardiologist and cardiac surgeon at a congenital heart disease centre. GP or primary care involvement is vital for annual vaccination reviews and for ensuring emergency stand-by antibiotics are renewed. When transitioning from paediatric to adult care (usually around age 16–18), a formal handover plan including the vaccination record is essential.
preventionStrategies: Complete all recommended vaccines on the earliest possible schedule; do not delay pneumococcal, meningococcal, or Hib vaccines.
Take daily prophylactic penicillin V without interruption; set a daily alarm if needed.
Carry emergency standby antibiotics and start them immediately on any fever above 38°C, then attend an emergency department.
Carry a medical alert card and wear a medical alert bracelet at all times.
Inform all healthcare providers — including dentists and pharmacists — of asplenia status before any procedure.
Avoid travel to malaria-endemic regions without specialist travel health advice, as asplenia increases the severity of malaria.
Receive pre-travel vaccinations (typhoid, hepatitis A, yellow fever where applicable) and seek specialist pre-travel advice.
lifestyleModifications: Never ignore a fever; treat any temperature above 38°C as a potential emergency and seek medical review the same day.
Keep vaccinations and booster records in a dedicated document and present them at every medical consultation.
Renew emergency standby antibiotic prescriptions proactively before expiry.
Inform school or workplace health services of the condition and the emergency action plan.

Avoid activities that significantly increase the risk of serious bacterial infection (e.g., working with animals without appropriate protection), though these restrictions are generally not required in everyday life.
Annual dental check-ups and prompt treatment of dental infections reduce one route of bacteraemia.

recommended: A balanced, nutrient-rich diet to support general immune health
Adequate vitamin D and zinc, which support innate immune function
Probiotic-containing foods such as yoghurt and kefir to support gut microbiome health
Sufficient hydration to maintain general health and reduce susceptibility to febrile illness
avoid: Raw shellfish and unpasteurised dairy products, which carry a higher risk of invasive bacterial infection in immunocompromised individuals
Excessive alcohol, which impairs immune function and can mask fever symptoms
Supplements that claim to 'boost' the immune system without evidence base — these do not substitute for vaccination and prophylaxis

exerciseGuidelines: People with congenital asplenia can participate in most sports and physical activities. Contact sports that risk splenic trauma are not a concern (there is no spleen to rupture), but sports with other injury risks should be assessed on their own merits. During any febrile illness, vigorous physical activity should be paused until recovery. Maintaining general physical fitness supports immune resilience and cardiovascular health, and is especially important for those who have undergone cardiac surgery in heterotaxy-related disease.
dailyManagement: Take prophylactic penicillin V (or prescribed alternative) every day at consistent times.
Keep emergency standby antibiotics — amoxicillin-clavulanate or the prescribed alternative — accessible at home and when travelling.
Check vaccination records annually with the GP or haematologist and schedule any due boosters.
Carry the medical alert card and ensure emergency contacts know the OPSI action plan.
Seek same-day medical attention for any fever above 38°C — do not wait overnight.
Inform new healthcare providers of asplenia at every new contact, including emergency, dental, and surgical consultations.
prognosis: With comprehensive vaccination, prophylactic antibiotics, and patient education, children and adults with congenital asplenia can expect a near-normal lifespan. The lifetime risk of OPSI in asplenic individuals is approximately 3–5% without prophylaxis; with prophylaxis it is substantially lower, though not zero. OPSI mortality when it does occur is approximately 50–70% even with treatment, highlighting that prevention rather than rescue is the strategy. In isolated congenital asplenia without heterotaxy, the prognosis is driven almost entirely by infection prevention adherence. In heterotaxy-related asplenia, the cardiac anatomy is the dominant prognostic determinant: children with complex single-ventricle physiology have greater long-term morbidity and mortality than those with correctable two-ventricle anatomy.
recoveryTimeline: Congenital asplenia is a lifelong condition with no cure. The protective effect of vaccines develops over the first months after immunisation. Prophylactic penicillin V provides day-to-day protection throughout the period it is taken. OPSI episodes, when they occur, are acute and require hospitalisation with intravenous antibiotics; recovery takes 5–14 days in survivors. Transition from paediatric to adult care at approximately 16–18 years is a high-risk period when adherence to prophylaxis can lapse; a formal written transition plan is recommended by clinical guidelines.
complications: Overwhelming post-splenectomy infection (OPSI) — rapidly fatal septicaemia from encapsulated bacteria; mortality approximately 50–70% even with treatment; S. pneumoniae causes the majority of cases.

Babesiosis and malaria — parasitic infections of red blood cells that the spleen normally controls; can cause severe haemolytic anaemia in asplenic patients, including from low-parasitaemia Babesia not detected on routine smear.

Capnocytophaga canimorsus septicaemia — a rare but dramatic infection following dog bites, capable of causing fulminant sepsis in asplenic individuals; dog bites must be treated with prompt antibiotics.
Thrombocytosis and associated thromboembolic events — persistent elevated platelet counts may slightly increase thrombosis risk, though clinical thrombosis is uncommon.
Complications of associated congenital heart disease — in heterotaxy-related asplenia, cardiac failure, arrhythmia, and surgical complications are the dominant long-term morbidities.

Antibiotic resistance — prolonged penicillin prophylaxis can select for resistant organisms in the individual and contribute to community resistance; periodic culture and sensitivity testing is recommended if breakthrough infections occur.

supportResources.name: Asplenia Network (UK)
supportResources.description: UK patient support and information resource for people living without a spleen, offering guidance on vaccination, antibiotics, travel, and emergency planning.

supportResources.name: British Society for Haematology — Asplenia Guidelines
supportResources.description: The definitive UK clinical guideline for preventing and managing infection in asplenic and hyposplenic patients, updated 2022.

supportResources.name: CDC — People at Risk of Serious Illness from Pneumococcal Disease
supportResources.description: CDC guidance on pneumococcal vaccination for high-risk groups including asplenic individuals.

sources.title: William BM, Corazza GR. Hyposplenism: a comprehensive review. Part I. Basic concepts and causes. Hematology 2007.
Bolze A et al. Ribosomal protein SA haploinsufficiency in humans with isolated congenital asplenia. Science 2013.
British Society for Haematology. Prevention and treatment of infection in patients with an absent or dysfunctional spleen (2022 update).
Rubin LG, Schaffner W. Clinical practice: care of the asplenic patient. New England Journal of Medicine 2014.
CDC. Updated recommendations for use of pneumococcal vaccines. MMWR 2023.
Brancaleoni V et al. Laboratory diagnosis of thalassemia. Int J Lab Hematol 2016.
Belmont JW. Genetics of laterality defects. Pediatr Cardiol 2007.

confusedWithConditions.name: Sickle cell disease
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Sickle cell disease causes functional asplenia through repeated splenic infarction in early childhood — a structurally abnormal spleen is present initially but auto-infarcts. Congenital asplenia has no spleen from birth and is a structural, not functional, disorder; the haematological picture differs markedly.

confusedWithConditions.name: Beta-thalassemia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Beta-thalassemia causes splenomegaly (an enlarged, overactive spleen) from haemolysis, the opposite of asplenia. They can both produce haematological abnormalities but the clinical presentations and management differ completely.

confusedWithConditions.name: Lymphoma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Lymphoma involving the spleen can cause hyposplenism through splenic destruction, but this is acquired rather than congenital, occurs in adults with constitutional symptoms and lymphadenopathy, and is diagnosed by biopsy rather than genetic testing.

relatedConditions.name: Sickle cell disease
relatedConditions.relevance: The most common cause of acquired functional asplenia; shares identical OPSI risks and infection prevention requirements as structural asplenia.

relatedConditions.name: Lymphoma
relatedConditions.relevance: Splenomegaly from lymphoma and post-splenectomy hyposplenism after splenectomy for staging or treatment produce similar immunological deficits to congenital asplenia.

relatedConditions.name: Pneumonia
relatedConditions.relevance: Streptococcus pneumoniae pneumonia is the most common and most dangerous infection in asplenic patients; prevention of pneumococcal pneumonia is the primary goal of asplenia management.

faqs.question: What is congenital asplenia?
faqs.answer: Congenital asplenia means a person is born without a spleen. The spleen filters bacteria from the blood and makes certain antibodies, so its absence leaves a lifelong vulnerability to sudden, rapidly fatal blood infections from encapsulated bacteria such as Streptococcus pneumoniae.

faqs.question: How is congenital asplenia diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis combines an abdominal ultrasound confirming the absence of splenic tissue with a peripheral blood film showing Howell-Jolly bodies — nuclear remnants in red blood cells that a normal spleen would remove. A full blood count typically shows elevated platelets and white cells.

faqs.question: What is the main risk of having no spleen?
faqs.answer: The main risk is overwhelming post-splenectomy infection (OPSI) — a septicaemia from capsulated bacteria that can kill within 24 to 48 hours even in previously well people. Streptococcus pneumoniae causes the majority of cases. With vaccination and prophylactic antibiotics, the risk is substantially reduced but not eliminated.

faqs.question: What vaccines do people with congenital asplenia need?
faqs.answer: People with congenital asplenia need vaccines against the three main encapsulated pathogens: pneumococcal conjugate and polysaccharide vaccines (with boosters every 5 years), meningococcal ACWY and B vaccines, Haemophilus influenzae type b vaccine, and annual influenza vaccine. Conjugate vaccines are preferred over plain polysaccharide vaccines for better immune response.

faqs.question: Should people with no spleen take antibiotics every day?
faqs.answer: Yes, most guidelines recommend daily prophylactic penicillin V (or amoxicillin) throughout childhood and — in the BSH 2022 guideline — lifelong. Taking it every day protects against pneumococcal bacteraemia on the days when the vaccine response may not be complete. People allergic to penicillin are given an alternative antibiotic.

faqs.question: What should I do if I have no spleen and develop a fever?
faqs.answer: Any fever above 38°C in an asplenic person is a medical emergency. Take your emergency standby antibiotics immediately and go to an emergency department the same day — do not wait overnight. Tell the triage nurse that you are asplenic. Sepsis can develop within hours and waiting can be fatal.

faqs.question: Is congenital asplenia hereditary?
faqs.answer: Isolated congenital asplenia linked to RPSA gene mutations follows autosomal dominant inheritance, so a parent with the mutation has a 50% chance of passing it to each child. Heterotaxy-associated asplenia can follow autosomal dominant, autosomal recessive, or X-linked patterns depending on the causative gene. Genetic counselling is advised for all affected families.

faqs.question: Can you live a normal life with no spleen?
faqs.answer: Yes. With consistent vaccination, daily prophylactic antibiotics, emergency standby medication, and appropriate awareness, most people with congenital asplenia live full and active lives. The key is never becoming complacent about fever and maintaining vaccines and prophylaxis throughout life.

faqs.question: What are Howell-Jolly bodies and why do they matter?
faqs.answer: Howell-Jolly bodies are small nuclear remnants inside red blood cells. A normal spleen removes them. When they are present on a blood film in anyone who has not had a splenectomy, they indicate absent or severely impaired splenic function and should prompt imaging to confirm asplenia and initiate infection prevention measures.

faqs.question: Do I need special precautions before surgery or dental procedures?
faqs.answer: Yes. Always inform your surgeon, anaesthetist, or dentist that you are asplenic before any procedure. Bacteraemia can occur during invasive dental procedures, so antibiotic prophylaxis may be recommended. Ensure your vaccination status is current before elective surgery.

faqs.question: Is travel safe for people without a spleen?
faqs.answer: Travel is generally safe with careful planning. Avoid malaria-endemic areas if possible, or seek specialist travel health advice well in advance. Malaria causes unusually severe illness in asplenic patients. Ensure all travel vaccines are current and carry sufficient emergency antibiotics for the duration of the trip.

faqs.question: What is the difference between congenital asplenia and functional asplenia?
faqs.answer: Congenital asplenia means no spleen was ever present, as a result of a genetic defect during fetal development. Functional asplenia means a spleen is structurally present but works poorly or not at all — as in sickle cell disease, where the spleen is destroyed by repeated infarction. Both conditions produce identical infection risks and require the same prevention measures.

faqs.question: What is heterotaxy syndrome and how does it relate to asplenia?
faqs.answer: Heterotaxy syndrome is a condition where the internal organs are not arranged in the normal left-right pattern. Right isomerism — bilateral right-sidedness — is associated with congenital asplenia because the spleen is a left-sided organ. Most children with heterotaxy-associated asplenia also have complex congenital heart defects requiring surgery.

faqs.question: How often should vaccines be boosted in asplenic patients?
faqs.answer: Pneumococcal polysaccharide vaccine (PPSV23) should be boosted every 5 years. Meningococcal ACWY boosters are given every 5 years in the UK and US guidelines. Annual influenza vaccination is recommended every year. The exact schedule should be personalised by a haematologist or immunologist based on the patient's vaccination history.

faqs.question: What is OPSI and how dangerous is it?
faqs.answer: OPSI stands for overwhelming post-splenectomy infection. It is a rapidly progressing bloodstream infection — most often from Streptococcus pneumoniae — that can develop from mild fever to septic shock within hours. Even with intensive care, mortality is approximately 50–70%. Prevention through vaccination and daily prophylaxis is far more effective than treatment.

faqs.question: Does being asplenic affect my ability to fight off colds and viruses?
faqs.answer: Congenital asplenia specifically impairs defence against encapsulated bacteria — pneumococcus, meningococcus, and Hib — rather than viruses. Common viral illnesses such as colds, influenza, and COVID-19 are not dramatically more severe in asplenic people, though influenza vaccination is recommended because influenza can lead to secondary bacterial pneumonia.

faqs.question: Can animal bites be more dangerous without a spleen?
faqs.answer: Yes. Dog and cat bites carry a risk of Capnocytophaga canimorsus infection — a bacterium that causes fulminant sepsis in asplenic individuals. Any animal bite in a person without a spleen should be thoroughly cleaned, and a clinician should be consulted promptly about antibiotic prophylaxis within hours of the bite.

faqs.question: Should asplenic patients avoid contact sports?
faqs.answer: Since there is no spleen to rupture, there is no specific restriction on contact sports for asplenic individuals on that basis. Activities can be chosen based on general health and fitness. People with associated congenital heart disease should follow specific cardiac activity guidelines from their cardiologist.

faqs.question: How does congenital asplenia affect platelet counts?
faqs.answer: The spleen normally sequesters approximately 30% of circulating platelets. Without a spleen, platelets remain in circulation, causing thrombocytosis — a persistently elevated platelet count, often in the range of 500,000 to 900,000 per microlitre. Clinical thrombosis from this thrombocytosis is uncommon in otherwise healthy individuals.

faqs.question: What genetic test confirms congenital asplenia?
faqs.answer: Sequencing of the RPSA gene identifies mutations in approximately 10–20% of isolated congenital asplenia cases. A broader laterality gene panel is used when heterotaxy is present to look for variants in ZIC3, NODAL, CFC1, and ciliopathy genes. Not all cases have an identifiable mutation with current testing panels.

metaTitle: Congenital Asplenia: Symptoms, Causes & Care | aihealz
metaDescription: Congenital asplenia means being born without a spleen, raising lifelong risk of fatal bacterial infections. Learn vaccines, antibiotics, and emergency steps.
